speaker: interviewee
text: They coming?

speaker: interviewer
text: You can come in. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, we just want a thumbnail sketch.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: From '56, you know, you can end with college, got married, graduate degrees, children.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape four, February 24, 1995, survivor Marianne Cooper. Please continue. It's now 1956. You, your mother, sister, and her family are getting ready to leave Hungary, and briefly bring us forward to the present day.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. My sister and her family left in October of-- of '56. My mother and I left on December 31st of 1956, which was basically the last day you could leave. The borders were being shut already. Walked across the border between Austria and Hungary, literally walked on frozen ground [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] for about 10 miles. Didn't think I was going to make it. Didn't think my mother was going to make it. We both made it. Think of over 50 women making it. That's something that is quite remarkable in my book. Got to Austria. The Red Cross was waiting for all the Hungarians as they were arriving. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This was an experience that my mother arranged. It should be noted that this is something that, to get the guides, we again paid money. This was not something that was done haphazardly. This was a done by guides who figured out what was the best time to do it, and that cost money. We got to the Red Cross in a small town in Austria. And then at that point, everybody was identified-- Jews non-Jews, who was going to be the major organization, social organization that was going to be helping you. Jews went with HIAS. And those who also were either students or professional people were also registered with the IRC, International Rescue Committee, which was specifically for, quote, 'intellectuals and students.' We eventually, through the help of HIAS, got into Vienna, and spent the next four months in Vienna. Four months, because the American government decided-- Mr. McCarthy was in office, and he decided no more refugees. And the only way this happen is that, eventually, families were united.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was a good thing that my sister left, because my mother could join my sister. And at that point, 21 was the age at which you become-- became of age. And since I was 19, at that point, I could come with my mother as her under each child, which was a lucky thing for me.

speaker: interviewee
text: We arrived in New York, and from New York immediately went to Syracuse. New York, because this is where my sister was, because my brother-in-law was offered a job by carrier. He's an engineer. We went in Syracuse. The Jewish family service took care of us there, and they got you settled. They helped you with a monthly stipend-- or weekly. I don't remember. I was--

speaker: interviewer
text: I was-- lucky for me, I was speaking English, because I've had English instruction from the time I was about seven-years old, right after the war. Why did I have that? Because we had relatives in this country, as I mentioned earlier. My mother had an aunt and uncle, and I had to learn English. So I was lucky I was able to speak English.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was also lucky because Syracuse University gave me a scholarship, which meant it was a full-tuition scholarship, nothing else. So I had no problems with tuition, but I had to earn my own living, which my mother and I did. I never lived on campus. I always lived with my mother. As a graduate of a European high school, I was given one year credit. So I was never a freshman. I started as a sophomore, which I think is interesting. And it is interesting that, because of this, I ended up graduating at the same time had I gone in Hungary. I graduated in 1960, just at my age group and age when I should have graduated.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I think it's interesting to note that twice I got away with something. I went to first grade for eight weeks, and I was pronounced finished. And then I came here, and I got out of college in three years as opposed to four years.

speaker: interviewee
text: I have decided, while I was at Syracuse University, that I was going to become a librarian. I worked in the library while I was in college. I graduated Phi Beta Kappa, cum laude. And I decided to become a librarian because of several reasons. A, it was going to take me full-time, another year to become a professional. And B, because this was a profession that I thoroughly investigated, and I thought that was something I really wanted to do. And that was a very good decision. I have never regretted it for one minute. I came to New York. I got my master's degree in New York, from Columbia. I met my husband while I was at Columbia through a blind date and got married three months after I met him. And we both got our master's degrees at the same time. He's an engineer. Got his master's from City College, and I got my master's from Columbia. I have begun to work. I got a job at Columbia. I was the chemistry librarian at Columbia from '61 to '66, which is the point in time that I left because my daughter was born. I worked all the way through being pregnant. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: After she was born, six months after, I started going for my doctorate, also at Columbia. Got a part-time job, went to school, and had a baby, and was greatly helped by my mother, who was watching the baby. Oh, god, would you put the phone back? [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] [BACKGROUND NOISE] They picked it up. They picked it up. OK. Keep going.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. I had a second baby. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] At that point, I stayed home and had gone on with my studies and completed almost everything with-- for the Ph.D. I was writing my dissertation. Then in 1975, I got hired by Queens College to start teaching. And I started teaching at the Graduate School of Library and Information Studies in 1975 at Queens College. In the meantime, my mother was very, very ill. We moved out here from New York. We moved into this house in '69 with two children-- a baby and a three-year-old. And my mother moved with us, and she was quite sick. She passed away in '77, lots of complications. At that point, so I was teaching. I finished my doctorate, finally-- defended in 1980-- and have been progressing at Queens. At this point, I am the Chairman, Director of the Graduate School of Library and Information Studies. And celebrate-- will celebrate my 20th year at Queens College. I am a very involved professional. Being a professional person means a lot to me. I am participating in many professional activities. I give papers at conferences. I write a lot of articles. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I am extremely involved with what I do. I also brought up two daughters. One daughter went to Columbia, has a bachelor's degree from Columbia. First woman's class at Columbia-- the first class that had women in it. Also has a master's degree in political science from Columbia, and works for the Chinese Cultural and Information Center in Rockefeller Center. My younger daughter went through NYU, has a bachelor's degree in journalism and a master's degree in media. She is pregnant right now so she's going to make us grandparents in July.

speaker: interviewer
text: So life has gone on and has become very full, and very nice, and very enjoyable with all of its pleasures and problems. And I feel thoroughly Americanized. I don't feel like a Hungarian at all. And I look at myself as an American Jew with a very strong Hungarian background. And hope that's the way I will continue.

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- as a point of interest, I understand I speak no language without an accent, at this point, because as-- as my English accent, and so is my Hungarian. This is something that I have been told several times. So I think it's an interesting point of departure. I think hearing stories of younger survivors of Holocaust, I think is very important. I also should say that, because I never was in a concentration camp, I didn't really look at myself as a survivor. And the reason I started changing my thinking is that my daughter's view of life and my daughter's really convincing me that I am a survivor. And it's-- I find it's very interesting that they are the ones who look at me as such. I went to a couple of meetings of Hidden Children. A few years ago, there was a lot of activity as to what is happening in this whole area of Hidden Children. And I have to say, I did not connect with them. I did not feel that any of my background relates to their background and-- and ties into that. So I went to two or three meetings, and then I did not go after that. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I don't know whether other Hungarian people who are my generation have the same experience-- or whether you will have a chance to talk to any or several of them. But we really have a very unique place, I think. And my daughter's convinced me that that's the case. And what's interesting is I had a chance to meet the director of research for the Holocaust at Queens College a couple of months ago. And we sat around in a wrap session and talked, and I asked him why there is so little information about this generation, and specifically, the Hungarians. And he acknowledged that there is very little, and they found the other stories were more important and had more significance and more reason to be preserved. And he felt that, if there was going to be a chance to have something done, this was the area that should be done.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I am quite delighted that this is going on. Because at this point, I decided that there is a story to be told from my perspective as well. OK. Thank you. You told it very well.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Cooper, please introduce your family to us.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. On the far right is my husband, Herb, of over 33 years. Next to me, immediately on my right, is my younger daughter, Evelyn. To my left is my older daughter, Debbie, and her husband, Ken Schapiro. One of our son-in-laws, Stewart Finkelstein, is missing, because he has the flu and so he's unable, unfortunately, to join us, even though he would have liked it. I think we are indeed very lucky that our family has expanded, and we are very proud and very pleased. And we are also very proud that we're going to be grandparents in about four and 1/2 months. So the family is really on its way of further expansion, and that's the way life goes on, which is very gratifying. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: I don't know if anybody would like to say anything. And if they do, who would like  to start? If they don't? Herb, you want to say something?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think you've heard the story of a remarkable and a very good lady-- a survivor. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Sometimes we've wondered whether God was asleep during the war. We do wonder why bad things happen to good people. But in the end, goodness did win. In the end, there were survivors. There was an awful cost to the ones who did not survive, and they won't be forgotten. But thank goodness, Marianne did survive. And through our children and their children, our people will go on.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'll-- I'll speak. I think just the fact that my mother didn't acknowledge, or didn't want to acknowledge, or whatever the case may be, that she was, in fact, a survivor is actually what shaped her whole being, I think, and therefore a lot of our being and all of our relationships together. Because I think that fact, unto itself, made her the hardest worker and just so focused on everything else that she did, that there was no time to dwell on anything else.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I think maybe now that this many years have passed, this is the time to, you know, more acknowledge, especially with the movie and all the attention that it's getting, I think now is the to look at it more closely. So it might have actually helped. Who knows? Who will ever know.

speaker: interviewee
text: I just wanted to say that I'm very, very proud of my mother. I've always been, but I had serious doubts that she would actually go through with this project. She gave a lot of different reasons for why it wasn't a great ideal to do it, and it wasn't necessary, and it was going to be too much high tech, and it was a waste of time, and she wasn't a classic survivor because she wasn't in a camp, and a lot of other reasons. But I'm so glad that you actually did it. And it's a very important story that needs to be told from as many perspectives as possible. And I've been proud of lots of things you've done during my lifetime, and I'm very proud that you were able to tell the story-- a difficult story-- of what you endured before you got to the US. And I love you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Wow.

speaker: interviewee
text: We all do.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, of course, we all do.

speaker: interviewee
text: Are you going to remain silent or you don't have to? Good, OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, I think one of the things that the Jewish people are and should be the proudest is that, after all these things, and for hundreds and hundreds of years, somehow we survived, and we go on. And we maintain ourselves, and we change with the times. But I would like to hope that we will be around for many hundreds of years to come. And that everybody-- as I was brought up with the story that, if God put everybody down and told them to get their package down and then he took everything and shook them all up and then said, OK, come back and pick up any package you want, everybody would go back to their own package automatically. I think we all have a story. We are all unique, but the total experience is what makes us Jews. And I think that's something that is very important. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This document is my father's diploma signifying that he passed the bar. His name was Dr. Laslo Abonyi. The Hungarian order is last name first, Abonyi Laslo. This is something that's interesting. Hungarians were assimilated. His father changed the name from [? Leichner, ?] which was the Jewish original family name, to Abonyi.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why Abonyi? Because they lived near a town whose name was Abony, A-B-O-N-Y. Abonyi became as the man from Abony. So that, in Hungar-- in Hungary, lawyers used the title, doctor, just like doctors use it everywhere. So it's Jurisprudence Doctor. That's what it symbolizes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is his diploma, dated 1936. It's fairly late that's because he didn't start going to college until much later since he was a prisoner of war in Siberia for six years. And by the time he came back and picked up his life, it took him a while. But in 1936, he finished and graduated. This is his diploma. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] This is my father as a young man, a member of the Austria-Hungarian army. That's the uniform. And this is at-- before he was taken as prisoner of war and taken to Siberia. As I mentioned, he spent close to six years there. That's it. The other two pictures--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Hold on one second. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] The one on top?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's taken at a labor camp where he was working from roughly '40, '41, I believe. No later than '42. This was inside Hungary. This is where he was taken. This is a, I believe, a bridge building activity. This is not where the slaughterhouse was. This is another forced labor camp. You can see him and many of the people he was with.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you, in the two pictures, just quickly point to which one is your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: That's all right. Oh, it's OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: It will follow you.

speaker: interviewee
text: [POINTS] This is my father here, with the sunglasses. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And this is my father down here. [POINTS] This picture also is interesting to me. This was also taken at one of these forced labor camps where he was at, and notice that there are two Hungarian soldiers there in the crowd with the men who were the laborers.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, it's very interesting to me that they would get into a picture. And if you did not know otherwise, it certainly might not seem like laborers in a forced labor camp because of the way the picture was taken and the group was put together. I find is absolutely fascinating. At least in the one on top, you see them in a work environment. Here they are lounging on the ground, very different. But I'm glad I have these two pictures. Hold on one second. Go to the top, and OK, now. Sorry.

speaker: interviewee
text: The top picture is a very formal family portrait, I believe, of the four of us-- myself, my sister, and my parents-- somewhere in Budapest. I don't remember where, but it certainly reflects a upper-middle class, very comfortable family in 1940. Very much part of the scene of the day that was taking place at the time in Budapest.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. The bottom one?

speaker: interviewee
text: The bottom picture-- I was waiting for you to say-- the bottom picture, also from 1940, was taken at Lake Balaton, which is the Hungarian sea where everybody spends their vacation. And again, reflects a very happy, middle-class family taking their vacation. In 1940, there was nothing that Hungarian Jews from Budapest were having problems with. There were no problems, as far as I could tell, judged by these pictures.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go.

speaker: interviewee
text: [POINTS] This is my third grade, end of the school year picture. This is me over here. I guess I was eight. This is the teacher and this is the class. Very nice memories of the class, and that's when life, sort of, was back to normal, after the war and after liberation. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, go.

speaker: interviewee
text: The bottom picture is my graduation picture, 1956. Graduation from high school. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My memory of myself wanting to look extremely cool-- that's what the picture reflects to me and that's the way I remember myself at that time. A period of life finished, completed.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: On top of the picture, you will see two of the yellow starts that two of us in the family wore. I don't remember which was mine and which was whose, but these are two that I have that we brought out with us when we left Hungary in '56. The bottom document actually was prepared in '58 at the request of my mother, and that document was prepared by the Hungarian Jewish Central Office, I guess that would be the translation. On top, you would see Israelites. And that document states that my mother and myself were restrained because of the law against Jews and were required to wear a Jewish star from 199-- from April 5, 1994. And at that time, because of the law, we also were among the families who had to move into designated Jewish houses, and that's where we lived until liberation. It also states, on the bottom, that my father was also there with us at that time. Of course, my father has deceased in '56. OK. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I think I made a mistake while I was describing the document and stated the year as being 1994 instead of 1944. The year, correctly, is 1944, when all of these took place, wearing of the Jewish star and being forced to live in the designated Jewish houses.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is one of the protection documents that was issued by the Swiss Consulate in Budapest, stating that the people whose name is indicated were protected by the Swiss government and they-- although, while they were not citizens, they were under the protection of the Swiss government and nothing could happen to them as far as deportation or any other act of atrocity that the Germans and their Hungarian allies were carrying on among the Jews. This was issued October 25, 1944. This was very important in our survival at that time. Protected us and contributed to our being saved.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This document was issued by the Bar Association of Budapest, and it's interesting. It was issued in '62, again, at the request of my mother. And this states that my father was a member of this Bar Association from 1936, April 21, until April 14, 1944. At that time, he was suspended because of Law Number 1210/1944ME because Jews were no longer allowed to practice law in Hungary. He then became a member of the Bar Association again in 1945, and practiced law until 1956, at which time he passed away. This is very important as an indication of what happened to professionals during the Nazi occupation in Hungary.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, go.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was a portrait-- wedding portrait-- of my daughter, Evelyn, and her husband, Stuart Finkelstein, who is unable to be here with us today on February 24th because he has the flu. This is their wedding picture. He's also a lawyer, so, in some respect, the family tradition of lawyering is continuing.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. That's the end of the interview with Mary Ann Cooper. The end of interview. [INAUDIBLE]
